coredata.dc:title: Targeting graphene quantum dots to epidermal growth factor receptor for delivery of cisplatin and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Nasrollahi, Fatemeh
Koh, Yun Rui
Chen, Peng
Varshosaz, Jaleh
Khodadadi, Abbas Ali
Lim, Sierin
coredata.dc:description: The unique properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which include high loading capacity, excellent physiological stability, strong photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and facile production make them attractive nanomaterials for biomedical applications. In this work, GQDs have been explored as dual-functional targeted drug carriers and cellular bioimaging agents. The GQDs were conjugated to single chain variable fragment of antibody (scFv), which had been engineered with high affinity (B10) to epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), via amide covalent linkages (GQDs-scFvB10). The morphology and surface modification of GQDs were characterized by HRTEM, SDS-PAGE, FT-IR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. Western blot analysis along with the confocal imaging of EGFR-overexpressing breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) demonstrated the targeting functionality of scFvB10 after conjugation to the GQDs, as well as the potential application of GQDs-scFvB10 in targeted bioimaging. The surface of targeted GQDs had a high cisplatin (CDDP) loading capacity of 50% and a pH-dependent release with slower release rate at neutral conditions, which can reduce the commonly observed systemic toxicity of CDDP. The targeted CDDP-loaded nanocarriers ((CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10) exhibited significantly higher toxicity on MDA-MB-231 cells compared to non-targeted ones suggesting their efficient uptake through EGFR. In contrast, cells with saturated EGFR showed lower uptake and cytotoxic effect of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10, demonstrating selectivity of the nanocarriers towards EGFR-overexpressing cells. The scFvB10-functionalized GQD is a promising platform for targeted cellular imaging and delivery of CDDP through interactions with EGFRs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Fluorescence imaging
Cytotoxicity
Controlled release
Graphene quantum dots
EGFR

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug release from the surface of GQDs-scFvB10
In vitro imaging, cellular uptake, targeting, and cytotoxicity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images and XRD pattern of GQDs. (b) PL spectra of GQDs solution under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: photographs of aqueous dispersion of GQDs under visible light (left) and UV at 365 nm (right).

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) FT-IR spectra of GQDs, scFvB10 and GQDs-scFvB10. (c) Gel electrophoresis of Marker (lane 1), scFvB10 (lane 2), GQDs-scFvB10 (lane 3), scFvB10 under UV light (lane 4), GQDs-scFvB10 under UV light (lane 5). (d) PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs-scFvB10 at excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

PL spectra of GQDs and (CDDP)GQDs at different R values (WCDDP/WGQDS) when (a) water (b) DMF were used as the solvent of CDDP. (c) Zeta potential of aqueous dispersion of GQDs and (CDDP)GQDs at different R values. (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDDP, GQDs-scFvB10 and (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10. (e) FT-IR spectra of CDDP, GQDs-scFvB10 and (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10.

(a) Release of CDDP from GQDs-scFvB10 at two different pH values. (b) PL spectra of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 before drug release and after 30 h of drug release at two different pH values. (c) PL spectra of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 during drug release at four different time intervals at pH 5.5.

Confocal fluorescence images of MDA-MB-231 cells after incubation with (a) 20 μg ml−1 GQDs dispersion for 40 min, (b) 20 μg ml−1 GQDs-scFvB10 dispersion for 40 min, (c) 20 μg ml−1 GQDs-scFvB10 and 30 μg ml−1 free scFvB10 for 40 min. (Left: green fluorescence of GQDs, right: overlay fluorescence image of nucleus stained with blue DAPI and green GQDs), (d) SDS-PAGE image of marker (lane 1) and western blot images of scFvB10 (lane 2) and EGFR (lane 3) when scFvB10 was used as the primary antibody, and GQDs-scFvB10 (lane 4) and EGFR (lane 5) when GQDs-scFvB10 was used as the primary antibody (e) Relative viability of MDA-MB-231 cells after 48 h incubation with CDDP, GQDs-scFvB10, (CDDP)GQDs, (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 and (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 + scFvB10 at different CDDP concentrations. The *, Δ and ◊ indicate p < 0.05 versus (CDDP)GQDs group, (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 + scFvB10 group and (CDDP)GQDs group at the same drug concentration, respectively. The data are presented as mean±standard deviation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of steps for preparation of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 complex.
Reported loading capacity of CDDP-loaded nanocarriers and their drug release behavior.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: MNPs: magnetic nanoparticles, MWCNTs: multi-walled carbon nanotubes, SWCNTs: single-walled carbon nanotubes, MSNs: mesoporous silica nanoparticles, GO: graphene oxide.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDDP loaded inside internal surfaces of MSNs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDDP conjugated to carboxyl groups of GO.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CDDP covalently conjugated to amphiphilic tri-block copolymer.

head.ce:title: Targeting graphene quantum dots to epidermal growth factor receptor for delivery of cisplatin and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 70 Nanyang Drive, Block N1.3, 637457, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Catalysis and Nanostructured Materials Laboratory, School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, P.O. Box: 11155/4563, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Novel Drug Delivery Systems Research Centre
School of Pharmacy and Pharmaceutical Sciences
Isfahan University of Medical Sciences and Health Services

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NTU-Northwestern Institute for Nanomedicine, Nanyang Technological University, 50 Nanyang Drive, Block N3.1, 637553, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The unique properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which include high loading capacity, excellent physiological stability, strong photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and facile production make them attractive nanomaterials for biomedical applications. In this work, GQDs have been explored as dual-functional targeted drug carriers and cellular bioimaging agents. The GQDs were conjugated to single chain variable fragment of antibody (scFv), which had been engineered with high affinity (B10) to epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR), via amide covalent linkages (GQDs-scFvB10). The morphology and surface modification of GQDs were characterized by HRTEM, SDS-PAGE, FT-IR, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopies. Western blot analysis along with the confocal imaging of EGFR-overexpressing breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) demonstrated the targeting functionality of scFvB10 after conjugation to the GQDs, as well as the potential application of GQDs-scFvB10 in targeted bioimaging. The surface of targeted GQDs had a high cisplatin (CDDP) loading capacity of 50% and a pH-dependent release with slower release rate at neutral conditions, which can reduce the commonly observed systemic toxicity of CDDP. The targeted CDDP-loaded nanocarriers ((CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10) exhibited significantly higher toxicity on MDA-MB-231 cells compared to non-targeted ones suggesting their efficient uptake through EGFR. In contrast, cells with saturated EGFR showed lower uptake and cytotoxic effect of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10, demonstrating selectivity of the nanocarriers towards EGFR-overexpressing cells. The scFvB10-functionalized GQD is a promising platform for targeted cellular imaging and delivery of CDDP through interactions with EGFRs.

ce:section.ce:para: Cisplatin (CDDP) is the most efficacious platinum-based drug, which plays a crucial role in treating testicular, ovarian, bladder, lung, head, and neck cancers. However, the therapeutic potency of CDDP is affected by its nonspecific attack to both normal and cancerous cells and its partial aqueous solubility. The indiscriminate accumulation of CDDP in all tissues, along with its interaction with plasma proteins, cause severe dose-related systemic toxicity and side effects including nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity and ototoxicity [1–3].

Different nanocarriers such as liposomes [4], polymers [5–7], magnetic nanoparticles [8], gold nanoparticles [9], mesoporous silica [10], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [11,12], and graphene oxide [13], have been introduced for efficient delivery of CDDP to tumors. The functionalization of nanoparticles with targeting agents leads to active targeting of specific cancerous tissues and considerable reduction of CDDP side effects [14]. Although liposomes-CDDP formulations are under clinical investigations, the low lipid solubility of CDDP hinders high drug loading [15]. On the other hand, the cytotoxicity of inorganic nanomaterials, especially those containing heavy metal ions, limited their clinical application as CDDP carriers [14].

Among the inorganic nanocarriers, graphene derivatives such as graphene oxide (GO) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess low toxicity. Their high drug loading capacity has also been demonstrated, making them promising nanocarriers for delivery of anti-cancer drugs [16–21]. However, GQDs have several advantages over GO and other inorganic nanocarriers. The exceptional hydrophilicity of GQDs results in their high stability in physiological environments and provides a stable formulation for effective delivery of partially water-soluble drugs like CDDP, whereas GO requires further functionalization to be stable in physiological environments [22–25]. Additionally, the biocompatibility and very low in-vitro (~80% viability at 100 μg/ml) and in-vivo toxicity of GQDs, attributed to their ultra-small size (<10 nm), were reported in different investigations [26,27]. Therefore, GQDs have the desired properties as delivery system for CDDP. Moreover, in contrast to GO, GQDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence property and are known as new class of imaging probes with tunable photoluminescence [28,29]. Their biocompatibility and optical stability along with their low-cost and simple production distinguish them from commonly used materials in cellular imaging such as inorganic semiconductor quantum dots with high toxicity and organic dyes with photobleaching property [29,30].

Potential applications of GQDs in bioimaging and drug delivery have been reported. The efficient fluorescence property of GQDs was demonstrated by monitoring their cellular uptake without obvious toxicity showing their potential in bioimaging applications [29,31,32]. The ultra-high loading capacity of GQDs was also achieved for doxorubicin (DOX) and curcumin [17,18]. Selective drug delivery and imaging were reported for targeting insulin and NGF receptors, CD-44, as well as “αvβ3” integrins, by conjugating insulin, nerve growth factor (NGF) [33], hyaluronic acid (HA) [34] and RGD peptides [35] to GQDs, respectively. Real-time monitoring of cellular uptake and selective cell labeling were achieved by using folic acid as the targeting moiety [36].

Application of graphene-based nanoparticles as the carrier of CDDP was studied by Tian et al. Their non-toxic, GO-PEG showed high loading capacity for CDDP [13]. However, the effect of targeting of nanocarrier on uptake and cytotoxic efficiency of GO-PEG/CDDP was not evaluated. Sui et al. reported the only research on integration of GQDs and CDDP focusing on therapeutic effect of GQDs on killing cancerous cells [37]. Their prepared GQDs exhibited DNA cleavage activity and relative cytotoxicity on cancerous cells lines [38]. Consequently, addition of GQDs improved the anti-cancer activity of CDDP, and the overall cytotoxicity was sum of the toxicities of CDDP and GQDs.

Herein, we present a non-cytotoxic, targeted, GQD-based nanocarrier with dual-functionality for delivery of CDDP and cellular imaging. The GQDs were targeted with a single chain variable fragment of antibody (scFvB10) to the EGFR, which is highly expressed in many cancerous cells than the normal ones [39–41]. The prepared nanocarriers leverage the unique properties of GQDs such as high drug loading capacity, fluorescence property and physiological stability. Targeting of GQDs would be particularly important for delivery of CDDP to cancerous tissues and reducing its commonly observed systemic toxicity. In contrast to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody (160 kDa), the scFv antibody (25–30 kDa) lacks the Fc portion resulting in less steric hindrance during conjugation and better accumulation at targeted cancerous regions [42,43].

The small size, single-layer GQDs, were prepared in a low-cost, efficient and facile method [32], followed by conjugation with recombinantly produced scFvB10 through amide linkages (GQDs-scFvB10). Subsequently, the aquated positive charge species of CDDP, were loaded on GQDs-scFvB10 via electrostatic interaction and complexation with carboxylic groups of GQDs ((CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10), (Scheme 1 ). In contrast to liposomes, the GQDs-scFvB10 exhibited a high loading capacity for CDDP. Additionally, its high stability in physiological environments for several months, along with its simple production and negligible cytotoxicity, make it superior nanostructure for cellular imaging and carrier of CDDP compared with other inorganic nanomaterials. It was found that the GQDs-scFvB10 could be effectively taken-up by EGFR-overexpressing MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells through EGFR-mediated endocytosis. We demonstrate the dual capabilities of the targeted nanoparticle for cellular imaging and delivery of CDDP to MDA-MB-231 cells with considerably higher cytotoxicity compared to the non-targeted CDDP-loaded nanocarrier.

Cisplatin (CDDP), 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), hydroxylamine, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) for nucleic acid staining, paraformaldehyde, epidermal growth factor receptor, isopropyl β-d-1-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG), ampicillin, and 2XTY medium were purchased from Sigma. Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF) and HNO3 68% were obtained from Merck Chemical Co. Vulcan CX-72 carbon black was purchased from Cabot Corporation (Boston, USA) and fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's modified eagle media (DMEM), 0.25% trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), penicillin streptomycin (Pen Strep), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF) protease were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Dialysis membrane (molecular mass cutoff of 2 kDa) was obtained from SpectrumLabs Co. (Los Angeles, USA), and Amicon ultra centrifugal filter units (10 kDa) were from Merck Millipore (Darmstadt, Germany). MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell line was a kind gift from professor Kathy Luo. The gene for the production of scFvB10 was obtained from professor Gregory Adams laboratory at Fox Chase Cancer Center. Ultrapure water produced by a Milli-Q gradient water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, USA) was used in all experiments.

GQDs were prepared from CX-72 carbon black in an oxidation process using nitric acid [32]. CX-72 carbon black (0.22 g) was added to nitric acid (50 ml, 6 M) and the mixture was refluxed for 24 h at 135 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through qualitative filter paper and the filtrate was heated to evaporate the nitric acid. The obtained reddish-brown solid was dispersed in water followed by ultrafiltration using 10 kDa Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter devices at centrifugal rate of 10,000 rpm (15,000g) for 30 min. The filtrate containing <10 kDa GQDs were collected and dried as the standard sample used in the subsequent experiments.

The scFvB10 gene was provided by Gregory P. Adams of Fox Chase Cancer Center and was subsequently codon optimized by GeneArt. The pSYN1 plasmids, into which the codon-optimized scFvB10 gene was cloned, was a generous gift from James D. Marks of University of California San Francisco. The glycerol stock of TG1 E. coli transformed with plasmids containing scFvB10 genes was pre-inoculated in 2xTY media (50 ml) with glucose (2%) and ampicillin (0.1% w/v) at 30 °C for 14 h and was added into 1l culture in a shaking incubator (250 rpm). The gene expression was induced for 16 h at 20 °C by adding 0.5 mM IPTG when the optical density measured at 600 nm reached ~0.9. The cells were harvested by centrifuging the bacterial culture at 4,000g for 30 min. The cell pellet from every liter of culutre was resuspended in 25 ml periplasmic extraction buffer (200 gl−1 sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, 30 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0) supplemented with PMSF protease inhibitor (250 μl of 100 mM) and incubated on ice for 30 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 4,000g at 4°C for 30 min again to collect the supernatant. Additional step of resuspending the cell pellet in 25 ml osmotic shock buffer (5 mM magnesium sulfate) and PMSF protease inhibitor (250 μl of 100 mM), followed by subsequent incubation and centrifugation did not improve the preparation. The supernatants were buffer exchanged into Buffer A (20 mM phosphate, 0.5 M sodium chloride, pH 7.4) using 10 kDa MWCO Amicon ultrafiltration device. The solution was loaded on a HisTrap HP 5 ml column to purify the protein via immobilized metal affinity chromatography (IMAC) in a fast protein liquid chromatography (FPLC) system (GE Healthcare). The equilibration and elution were done using Buffer A and Buffer B (Buffer A + 500 mM imidazole), respectively. The eluate was collected and analyzed with bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay, sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and western blot.

To conjugate GQDs to scFvB10 antibody fragment, an EDC/NHS mediated reaction was performed and activated carboxyl groups of GQDs were linked to the amine groups of scFvB10. Briefly, a homogenous dispersion of GQDs in activation buffer (0.1 M 2-morpholino ethane sulfonic acid, 0.5 M NaCl, pH6.0) was prepared at 1 mg ml-1 followed by addition of EDC (20 mM) and NHS (20 mM). The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 1 h to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs. Subsequently, a solution of scFvB10 in conjugation buffer (0.1 M sodium phosphate, 150 mM NaCl, pH7.4) at 0.15 mg ml−1 was added to the GQDs dispersion and incubated at room temperature for 6 h. Finally, the coupling reaction was quenched by addition of hydroxylamine (20 mM). To separate the free unconjugated GQDs, the mixture was ultrafiltered through 10 kDa MWCO Amicon centrifugal filter devices at 7,500g for 30 min and the retentate was washed five times with phosphate buffered saline (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4).

The CDDP solution (5 ml, 0.4 mg ml−1) was prepared by dissolving CDDP powder in DI water at 60 °C and was added to the GQDs-scFvB10 solution (5 ml, 0.4 mg ml−1) to reach the CDDP and GQDs-scFvB10 concentrations of 0.2 mg ml−1. The mixture was stirred for 30 h at room temperature in the dark. The undissolved free CDDP molecules were removed through a 10 kDa MWCO filter and the retentate was washed thrice with water by centrifugation at 7,500g for 20 min. To measure the concentration of loaded CDDP, the retentate containing (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 dispersion, along with the prepared standard samples at platinum concentration range of 1.5–48 ppm, was sent for inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) analysis. The effect of the CDDP solvent on the loading capacity of the nanocarriers was also studied by dissolving CDDP in DMSO and DMF instead of DI water. The amount of loaded drug in the (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 dispersion was measured immediately after separation of free materials at the end of loading process using ICP method.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Philips CM300, Amsterdam, Netherlands) and X-Ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Karlsruhe, Germany) were employed to investigate the morphology, and the size of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles. TEM samples were prepared by placing the aqueous suspension of GQDs on the copper grids and drying under ambient condition. ζ potential of nanoparticles was measured using Zetasizer (Malvern, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Bruker Vector22, Ettlingen, Germany) spectroscopy on the samples in potassium bromide (KBr) pellets, along with the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Shimadzu UV 2450, Kyoto, Japan) were performed to study the conjugation of GQDs to scFvB10 and to characterize their functional groups. The successful conjugation was also confirmed by running electrophoresis at 120 V for 45 min on 10% SDS polyacrylamide gels. Western blot technique was performed on GQDs-scFvB10 to study the affinity of GQDs-scFvB10 to EGFR. An imaging system (ProXPRESS 2D, Pekin Elmer, Waltham, USA) was used to image the SDS-PAGE gels and nitrocellulose papers in western blot analysis. Fluorescence spectra of the nanocomplexes of GQDs were recorded using a spectrophotometer with multiple detection modes (SpectraMax M5 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader, Sunnyvale, USA), which was also used for the absorbance measurement of formazan crystals in the cell culture plates in MTT assay. A confocal laser scanning microscope (Carl Zeiss, LSM 510 Meta Laser, Jena, Germany) with a 405 nm laser was employed to observe the fluorescence images of the cells. ICP analysis was performed on the CDDP loaded nanocarrier using an ICP-MS instrument (Perkin Elmer, ELAN DRC-e, Waltham, USA) to measure the amount of loaded CDDP.

The (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 dispersion was divided into three equal aliquots, which were placed in three dialysis bags (2 kDa). Each dialysis bag was immersed in a release reservoir containing PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4), which was stirred at 37 °C in the dark. From each release reservoir, 3.5 ml of buffer was withdrawn at different time intervals and the same volume of fresh PBS was replaced. To measure the amount of released drug at each time interval, the collected samples along with prepared standard platinum samples at concentration range of 0.25–10 ppm were analyzed by ICP. The same experiment was repeated at pH 5.5.

The cell culture experiments were performed on the MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell line to investigate the cellular imaging and cellular toxicity of GQDs-scFvB10 and (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 samples. The MDA-MB-231 cells (passage number: 6) were kept in DMEM medium supplemented with FBS (10% (v/v)) and penicillin/streptomycin (1% (v/v)). The cells were seeded in the flasks and incubated at 37 °C under a humid 5% CO2 atmosphere.

To examine the potential application of GQDs in cellular imaging, the uptake of the nanocarriers in MDA-MB-231 cells was visualized using confocal laser microscopy. The cells were seeded in an eight-well confocal chamber (15,000 cells per well in 300 μl medium) and incubated for 24–30 h to reach 80% confluency. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing 20 μg ml−1 of samples (GQDs or GQDs-scFvsB10) and incubated for 40 min. The medium was subsequently removed and the cells were washed twice with the fresh medium. Then, the cells were stained with DAPI for 15–20 min, followed by washing with fresh medium twice and cold PBS twice. The cells were fixed for 10 min with 4% paraformaldehyde (100 μl), and washed for 2–3 times with PBS. The cells were kept in PBS at 4 °C and imaged using confocal microscope. To study the targeting functionality of scFvB10 after conjugation to GQDs, the same experiment was repeated for the cells whose EGFRs had been saturated by addition of pure scFvB10 (30 μg ml−1) for 20 min prior to the addition of GQDs-scFvB10.

The MDA-MB-231 cell viability after treatment with targeted and non-targeted CDDP-loaded GQDs was examined using MTT assay. The 96-well plates were seeded with cells (7,500 cells per well in 200 μl culture medium) and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the culture medium was replaced with fresh medium (180 μl), followed by addition of each sample in DI water (20 μl) in more than three wells. The samples included DI water as the negative control, GQDs-scFvB10, CDDP, (CDDP)GQDs, and (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 at final CDDP- concentrations of 5, 15, 35, and 75 μg ml−1. After 48 h of incubation, MTT solution (20 μl of 5 mg ml−1) was added to the wells, and the cells were incubated for another 3h. To dissolve the formed formazan crystals, DMSO (150 μl) was added to the wells after removal of the cell medium. The absorbance of each well at wavelength of 570 nm was determined using a microplate reader, and the relative cell viabilities were calculated. To investigate the uptake mechanism of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10, the same experiment was repeated for cells incubated with scFvB10 (30 μg ml−1) for 20 min, followed by addition of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 at final CDDP-concentrations of 15 and 75 μg ml−1. Cell toxicity experiments for each sample was repeated for at least thrice in triplicate.

Statistical analysis was carried out by employing the SPSS software (ver.18, USA), and the statistical differences were analyzed using the student's t-test method. Differences were considered as statistically significant when the p-values were < 0.05. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviations.

A low-cost, high-yield, and one-step facile method was employed to prepare small-size, single-layer GQDs, by chemically oxidizing CX-72 carbon black, followed by an ultrafiltration process following a published method [32]. The transparent yellow color GQDs aqueous dispersion, was highly stable for several months and showed excellent colloidal stability in simulated physiological environments such as cell medium and phosphate buffered saline (PBS).

Characterizations using HRTEM (Fig. 1a) showed fairly uniform round-shaped GQDs with diameter of <5 nm, high crystallinity and lattice spacing of 0.27 nm, corresponding to the hexagonal lattice structure. The height profile of GQDs, obtained from AFM images showed a thickness of <2 nm, suggesting the single or few-layered sheets of GQDs (Supplementary Information (SI), Fig. S1) [16]. The resulting GQDs showed typical XRD pattern with a sharp peak (002) centered at 21.4 degrees [32].

To examine the possible application of GQDs in cellular imaging, the fluorescence properties were evaluated by investigating their photoluminescence (PL) behavior at different excitation wavelengths. Similar to carbon quantum dots [44], GQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent emission, and their PL intensity decreased remarkably by increasing excitation wavelength from 350 to 600 nm (Fig. 1b). The strongest PL appeared at excitation wavelength of 350 nm centered at emission wavelength of 510 nm. As shown in Fig. 1b (inset), the aqueous, yellow-color, GQDs solution emitted a strong green PL when exposed to UV light, which could be easily observed by naked eyes. The results suggest a potential application of GQDs as imaging agent.

The GQDs were functionalized with scFvB10 for specific drug delivery and cellular imaging of cancerous cells with high expression of EGFR. scFvB10 was conjugated to GQDs by formation of amide bond between the amine groups on scFvB10 and the carboxyl groups of GQDs in an EDC/NHS mediated reaction. The EGFR-specific antibody fragments with higher affinity towards EGFR than other antibodies [45], were prepared recombinantly. Detailed results can be found in the Supplementary Information (SI, Figs. S2 and S3).

To confirm the conjugation of scFvB10 to GQDs, we performed UV–vis and FT-IR spectroscopies. Fig. 2a shows that the GQDs strongly absorb UV light at wavelengths of <300 nm. They exhibited two characteristic broad absorption peaks at 231 and 360 nm, which are attributed to the π − π ∗ transitions of the aromatic CC bonds and n − π ∗ transitions of the CO bonds, respectively [46]. UV absorption of GQD solutions at 231 nm was used to measure the unknown concentration of GQDs (SI, Fig. S4). The scFvB10 antibody displayed the typical UV absorption peak of biological macromolecules at 280 nm, originating from the absorbance of tryptophan and tyrosine aromatic amino acids [47], and another apparent sharp absorption peak at 210 nm. The appearance of a new shoulder at 276 nm and a peak at 207 nm in the UV spectra of GQDs-scFvB10, which are assigned to the scFvB10, as well as blue shift of GQDs-UV absorption from 231 to 223 nm, confirmed successful conjugation of scFvB10 to GQDs.

The GQDs and GQDs-scFvB10 bioconjugates were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. Fig. 2b shows the presence of various oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs. The peaks arising from OH stretching vibrations, CC vibrations of aromatic rings, and CO vibrations of carboxyl groups can be observed at 3441 cm−1, 1616 cm−1 and 1691 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1427 cm−1, 1263 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 are also from the CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl, hydroxyl and epoxy groups [33]. The excellent stability of GQDs in aqueous and physiological environments is the result of the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of GQDs which led to the highly negative ζ potential of −31.3 mV in GQDs aqueous solutions (SI, Fig. S5a). Furthermore, the carboxyl functional groups of GQDs facilitated the conjugation of scFvB10 targeting agent to the surface of GQDs by formation of covalent amide linkages between carboxyl and amine groups. In the FT-IR spectra of GQDs-scFvB10, the appearance of new peaks at 1645 cm−1 and 1548 cm−1, which correspond to the CO and NH bending vibrations of amide bonds, as well as the new peak at 3128 cm−1 from NH vibrations, suggests the successful formation of the amide linkages. Additionally, the significant decrease or disappearance of CO stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups in GQDs at 1427 cm−1, and the shifts in other CO bonds of GQDs, confirm the covalent conjugation of scFvB10 to GQDs.

Conjugation of scFvB10 to GQDs was further visualized using gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) under UV light. Fig. 2c shows that the molecular mass of scFvB10 is around 26 kDa and GQDs-scFvB10 sample shows similar molecular mass as GQDs are only about 2 kDa. Under UV light, only samples containing GQDs exhibited fluorescence in the corresponding band of scFvB10 at 26 kDa. This observation suggests that the fluorescence property of GQDs-scFvB10 originates from the GQDs. Control sample without the EDC/NHS did not show emission at 26 kDa, and no fluorescence was observed in its PL spectra after separation of free GQDs, indicating no coupling in the absence of EDC/NHS (SI, Figs. S6a and S6b).

Fig. 2d shows the PL behavior of GQDs after conjugation to scFvB10. The fluorescence observed in the PL spectra of GQDs-scFvB10 is comparable to that of GQDs. This strong fluorescence suggests possible use of GQDs-scFvB10 as a fluorophore for targeted cellular imaging.

Leveraging on their physiological and aqueous stability, GQDs were explored as delivery vehicle for simple loading of CDDP, an anticancer drug with low water solubility. CDDP was loaded on GQDs-scFvB10 after a simple mixing process, followed by filtration to separate free drug molecules. To investigate the loading of CDDP on the surface of GQDs, the PL spectra of drug loaded GQDs and the effect of drug concentration on their loading capacity were evaluated. The loading of CDDP on GQDs is verified by the significant quench in GQD fluorescence after drug loading, at excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 3a). The fluorescence intensity reduced by increasing initial CDDP concentration at a constant GQDs concentration, indicating higher drug loading on GQDs surface at higher R values (WCDDP/WGQDS). ICP-MS analysis also showed that the loading capacity of GQDs increases from 18% to 50% by increasing R value from 0.25 to 1.

The drug loading experiment was repeated using other drug solvents to examine the effect of solvent on the amount of loaded drug. Although the fluorescence intensity decreased by increasing concentration of CDDP dissolved in DMF (Fig. 3b), the quenching of fluorescence in Fig. 3b is remarkably less than Fig. 3a. The observation correlates with the much lower loading capacity of <18% for R values of 0.25–4 (SI, Table S1). This suggests that the amount of loaded drug on the surface of GQDs was considerably higher when water was used as the solvent. As shown in Fig. S6c, similar result was obtained for DMSO. Consequently, water was used as the suitable solvent for CDDP in subsequent experiments.

The CDDP-loading capacity of GQDs is similar to the reported loading capacity of 58% for GO [13] and is much higher than the loading capacity in other nanocarriers such as nanotubes, magnetic nanoparticles, polymers, liposomes, and mesoporous silica reported at <20% (Table 1 ) [4–6,8,10–12,15]. Such a high loading capacity for GQDs and GO is a result of their high aspect ratio and possibility of drug loading on both sides. Furthermore, the efficient CDDP loading, particularly in aqueous solution, can be explained by hydrolysis of CDDP in water. Hydration of CDDP in water results in formation of positively charged dechlorinated species, which allows drug loading on the negative surface of GQDs through electrostatic interaction [48,49].

Fig. 3c and Fig. S5b (SI) show that the increase in the initial CDDP concentration in the drug loading experiment leads to the decrease of ζ potential while the aggregation of particles was observed at R values of >2. This is a result of neutralization of negatively charged functional groups of GQDs, such as carboxyl groups, by the positively charged CDDP molecules and confirms CDDP loading through electrostatic interaction. Although higher loading capacities were obtained at higher initial drug concentrations (SI, Table S1), the R = 1 with negative ζ potential of −25 mV and loading capacity of 50% was chosen as the standard protocol in the drug loading experiments to avoid aggregation and obtain the most stable formulation. Our prepared targeted GQDs had good stability without any precipitation in physiological solutions for months before and after drug loading. In contrast to CDDP which is insoluble in water at high drug concentrations, the prepared (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 exhibited good colloidal stability at the same concentrations suggesting its potential for aqueous formulation of CDDP.

UV–vis spectrum of targeted GQDs after drug loading is shown in Fig. 3d. In addition to the observed shoulder at 279 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10, originating from conjugated scFvB10, the appearance of another peak at 203 nm from UV absorption of CDDP indicated loading of CDDP on GQDs-scFvB10. To further examine the loading of CDDP on GQDs-scFvB10, FT-IR spectra of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 were compared with CDDP and GQDs-scFvB10. Fig. 3e reveals two new peaks at 1651 cm−1 and 1454 cm−1 which belong to the asymmetric and symmetric amine bending vibrations of CDDP [50]. The diminishing COOH peak of GQDs and its shift from 1708 cm−1 in GQDs-scFvB10 to 1697 cm−1 in (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 indicated the loading of CDDP on GQDs-scFvB10 through the complexation with carboxylic groups of GQDs. Formation of coordination complexes between active hydrated species of CDDP and various ligands like carboxylic acids were reported in other investigations [8,14,51]. The hydrogen bonding is another possible interaction between CDDP and functional groups of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release from the surface of GQDs-scFvB10
ce:section.ce:para: Release behavior of CDDP and its pH-dependency over a time period of 96 h at pH values of 7.4 and 5.5 are shown in Fig. 4a. The CDDP exhibited a significantly faster release at pH 5.5 with the release of 60% of the loaded drug after 6 h, while the same percentage of drug was released within 55 h at pH 7.4, and only 20% of the loaded drug was released after 6 h. The release behavior of CDDP in acidic conditions showed a burst release profile in the first 10 h, but the release rate gradually declined and reached a plateau, with 97% of the drug released in 72 h. In contrast, the CDDP was released at slower rate under neutral conditions with only 51% of the loaded drug released over 96 h. The faster release of CDDP at pH 5.5 was also confirmed in the PL spectra of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 after 30 h of drug release which revealed higher fluorescence intensity in the acidic conditions than the neutral conditions (Fig. 4b). The higher release rate can be attributed to the weaker electrostatic interaction between CDDP and GQDs-scFvB10 at acidic conditions, due to the protonation of functional groups of GQDs and neutralization of their negative charge. This situation also leads to the dissociation of possible hydrogen bonds between CDDP and GQDs-scFvB10, which are stronger in neutral conditions. The CDDP in the formed carboxylate-CDDP complexes can be released via acid mediated hydrolysis which facilitates the release of CDDP in acidic conditions.

The pH-sensitive, stimuli-responsive feature of different graphene-based nanostructures was reported in several investigations [16,35,52,53]. The extracellular tumor tissues and intracellular lysosomes and endosomes are acidic while the body fluid is neutral [35,54]. Therefore, the pH-dependent release behavior of CDDP is advantageous in reducing its systemic toxicity. A large portion of CDDP in an intravenous injection is bound to blood plasma proteins within the first day of administration resulting in systemic toxicity of CDDP [55]. Minimizing the release of CDDP in neutral pH is expected to reduce the interaction of free CDDP with plasma proteins leading to efficient cytotoxicity of drug against tumor tissues, which should be further investigated and confirmed in in-vivo experiments. The pH dependent, sustained release behavior of CDDP from GQDs-scFvB10 is in good agreement with reported investigations on the release of CDDP from the surface of graphene oxide and other nanocarriers (Table 1) [4–6,8,10–13,15].

The fluorescence property of nanocarrier after drug release was evaluated by recording the PL spectra of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 at desired time intervals during the drug release at pH 5.5. As expected, upon the release of CDDP, the fluorescence intensity of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 increased (Fig. 4c). As shown in Fig. 4c, the maximum fluorescence intensity of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 increased by three times after 48 h of drug release. This fluorescence recovery indicates potential simultaneous application of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 in drug delivery, monitoring the drug release behavior, and cellular imaging, after the release of CDDP in the targeted cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging, cellular uptake, targeting, and cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate their possible application in cellular imaging, the MDA-MB-231 cells were incubated with GQDs and the cells were observed with a laser-scanning confocal microscope. As shown in Fig. 5a, the green fluorescence of GQDs inside the cytoplasm and nucleus indicates their translocation through the cell membrane. Therefore, the uptake of the nanocarrier and its intracellular localization could be monitored using fluorescent GQDs. The confocal fluorescence images of the cells incubated with GQDs-scFvB10 in Figs. 5b and S7 show that fluorescence of GQDs is retained after conjugation with scFvB10 and increases by increasing concentration of GQDs and incubation time. The strong green fluorescence exhibits localization of GQDs-scFvB10 inside the cytoplasm and nucleus of cells. The considerably higher fluorescence intensity of GQDs-scFvB10 in Fig. 5b compared to GQDs in Fig. 5a at the same concentration of GQDs, demonstrates more efficient uptake of targeted GQDs.

Furthermore, the binding affinity of scFvB10 antibody in the GQDs-scFvB10 to the EGFRs which are over-expressed in the MDA-MB-231 cells, were evaluated. The green fluorescence observed in Fig. 5b for the cells incubated with GQDs-scFvB10 dramatically decreased when the EGFRs were saturated with excess free scFvB10 prior to incubation with the same amount of GQDs-scFvB10 (Figs. 5c and SI, S7). This finding also suggests targeting efficiency of scFvB10 to EGFRs and implies specific uptake of the GQDs-scFvB10 via EGFR on EGFR-overexpressing cells.

The functionality of scFvB10 in binding to the EGFR after conjugation with GQDs was further examined using western blot analysis. By using scFvB10 as the primary antibody, the EGFR band at 65 kDa confirmed binding of scFvB10 to EGFR (Fig. 5d, lane 3). The same result was obtained when the GQDs-scFvB10 was used as the primary antibody (Fig. 5d, lane 5). Binding of the secondary antibody to the His-tag on scFvB10 (primary antibodies) is shown by the 26 kDa bands (Fig. 5d, lanes 2 and 4). Western blot results along with the cellular confocal images confirmed the application of GQDs-scFvB10 in cellular imaging and its targeting ability and internalization via specific binding to the EGFR of the cells with high expression of EGFR.

The evaluation on cytotoxicity and targeting efficiency of CDDP-loaded nanocarrie were performed on MDA-MB-231 cells using MTT assay. The relative viability of MDA-MB-231 cells after 48 h incubation with the drug-free GQDs-scFvB10, and targeted and non-targeted CDDP-loaded nanocarriers at CDDP concentrations of 5, 15, 35 and 75 μg ml−1 are shown in Fig. 5e. A considerable enhancement in cell killing was achieved by the (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 compared with (CDDP)GQDs which can be explained by better uptake and cellular internalization of the targeted nanocarrier. The results are consistent with the confocal images of targeted and non-targeted GQDs in Fig. 5a and b. The (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 exhibited a significant (p < 0.05) decrease of about 25% in the relative cell viability compared with (CDDP)GQDs at CDDP concentrations of 15, 35 and 75 μg ml−1. The significantly (p < 0.05) lower toxicity of (CDDP)GQDs than free CDDP can be explained by the different mechanism of cellular uptake and release behavior of drug. Free CDDP directly enters the cells through a simple passive diffusion or by copper transporter proteins and reaches the nucleus in a short period of time [56,57]. However, the CDDP-loaded nanocarrier passes through the membrane via endocytosis [58], followed by time-dependent release of drug and diffusion inside the nucleus [34,35,59]. Similar cytotoxic effect was also reported for non-targeted GO-CDDP, however, the effect of GO targeting on cytotoxicity of CDDP was not studied [13]. The enhancement in the uptake of targeted GQDs led to a non-significant (p > 0.05) difference between cytotoxicity of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 and free CDDP, which is similar to the cytotoxicity behavior of GQDs-DOX targeted with HA, FA and RGD compared with free DOX in other investigations [34–36]. The drug-free GQDs-scFvB10 control revealed cell viability of >95%, indicating that the targeted nanocarrier was non-toxic and that the cytotoxicity originated from the loaded CDDP. Also, the GQDs showed viability of over 95% at 2–80 μg ml−1 on MDA-MB-231 cells, suggesting it as a suitable nanocarrier for CDDP in a wide range of concentrations (SI, Fig. S8). It is different from the findings of an investigation in which their prepared GQDs with larger lateral size (40 nm) and DNA cleavage activity created higher cytotoxicity (viability ~65% at 10 μg/ml) against different cancerous cell lines. As a result, addition of their GQDs to CDDP (GQDs + CDDP) enhanced cytotoxicity of CDDP originated from both GQDs and CDDP [37].

Cytotoxicity of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 on the cells whose EGFRs were saturated by the addition of excess free scFvB10 antibody, was also evaluated at the CDDP concentrations of 15 and 75 μg ml−1. The cytotoxicity was close to that of non-targeted (CDDP)GQDs but was significantly (p < 0.05) lower than that of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10. This observation confirmed the targeting effect of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 and its uptake mechanism through the EGFR-mediated endocytosis, which was also verified in the cellular imaging results in Figs. 5c and S7 in SI. Therefore, the GQDs-scFvB10 selectively targets the cells which express high level of EGFR. Since, the EGFR is highly expressed in many cancer types, the targeted nanocarrier would be selectively taken-up by cancerous cells, although the in-vivo experiments are necessary to study the targeting efficiency of GQDs-scFvB10 for imaging and delivery of CDDP to the cancerous tissues.

The GQD nanoparticles conjugated to the scFvB10 antibody were successfully synthesized for targeted delivery of CDDP and cellular imaging. The efficient uptake and targeting mechanism of GQDs-scFvB10 through the EGFR-mediated endocytosis, were verified using strong fluorescence of GQDs as bioimaging agents. Targeted delivery of CDDP was achieved by loading of CDDP on the GQDs-scFvB10 with a high loading capacity of 50%. The targeted, CDDP-loaded nanocarrier showed higher cytotoxicity on MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells, compared with the non-targeted one. The extreme stability of (CDDP)GQDs-scFvB10 even at high concentrations of CDDP, in which free drug is poorly soluble in aqueous solutions, would enhance the therapeutic efficiency of CDDP. Additionally, slower release of CDDP under neutral conditions along with its targeted delivery through the EGFRs is expected to increase its accumulation in cancerous tissues leading to potentially less systemic toxicity. The considerable fluorescence, extreme stability, simple production, no obvious cytotoxicity, high drug loading capacity, pH-dependent release and targeting efficacy demonstrate promising application of GQDs-scFvB10 for EGFR-targeted delivery of CDDP and bioimaging.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded partially by NTU-Northwestern Institute for Nanomedicine at Nanyang Technological University, Singapore. The authors thank Arun Kumar Prabhakar for his assistance with the initial GQDs preparation, Barindra Sana and Valerie Loh for the reconstruction of the plasmid containing scFvB10 gene.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2018.09.020.
